title: Movement Data Visualisation
text:
Without any background

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(movement, aes(x = X, y = Y)) +
  geom_path(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_point(colour = "red", size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Simulated Animal Movement",
    x = "X Coordinate",
    y = "Y Coordinate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Movement Data Visualisation
text:
With map background

# Install packages if needed
# install.packages(c("sf", "maptiles", "ggplot2", "dplyr", "ggspatial"))

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(sf)

Linking to GEOS 3.14.1, GDAL 3.12.1, PROJ 9.7.1; sf_use_s2() is TRUE

library(maptiles)
library(ggspatial)

#-------------------------------------------------
# Simulate animal movement
#-------------------------------------------------

set.seed(123)

n <- 120

movement <- data.frame(
  Time = seq.POSIXt(
    from = as.POSIXct("2025-01-01 08:00"),
    by = "30 min",
    length.out = n
  )
)

# Simulated random walk
movement$dx <- rnorm(n, mean = 0.001, sd = 0.003)
movement$dy <- rnorm(n, mean = 0.0005, sd = 0.002)

# Starting location (Colombo)
start_lon <- 79.8612
start_lat <- 6.9271

movement$Longitude <- start_lon + cumsum(movement$dx)
movement$Latitude  <- start_lat + cumsum(movement$dy)

#-------------------------------------------------
# Convert to sf object
#-------------------------------------------------

pts <- st_as_sf(
  movement,
  coords = c("Longitude", "Latitude"),
  crs = 4326,
  remove = FALSE
)

#-------------------------------------------------
# Download OpenStreetMap tiles
#-------------------------------------------------
# downloads map tiles from OpenStreetMap for the area covered by your movement points.
osm <- get_tiles(
  pts,
  provider = "OpenStreetMap",
  zoom = 13
)

#-------------------------------------------------
# Plot
#-------------------------------------------------

ggplot() +
  layer_spatial(osm) +
  geom_path(
    data = movement,
    aes(Longitude, Latitude),
    colour = "blue",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = movement,
    aes(Longitude, Latitude),
    colour = "red",
    size = 2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Simulated Animal Movement",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Movement Data Visualisation
text:
Example 1: OpenStreetMap (road map)

mapview(
  movement.sp,
  zcol = "Animal",
  map.types = "OpenStreetMap"
)

title: Movement Data Visualisation
text:
Example 2: Satellite imagery

mapview(
  movement.sp,
  zcol = "Animal",
  map.types = "Esri.WorldImagery"
)

title: Movement Data Visualisation
text:
Example 3: Topographic map

mapview(
  movement.sp,
  zcol = "Animal",
  map.types = "Esri.WorldTopoMap"
)

title: Movement Data Visualisation
text:
Example 4: Dark background

mapview(
  movement.sp,
  zcol = "Animal",
  map.types = "CartoDB.DarkMatter"
)

title: Movement Data Visualisation
text:
Example 5: Multiple basemaps (switch interactively)
You can provide several maps:

mapview(
  movement.sp,
  zcol = "Animal",
  map.types = c(
    "OpenStreetMap",
    "Esri.WorldImagery",
    "Esri.WorldTopoMap"
  )
)





Then the map viewer will allow you to switch between:
Road map Satellite map Terrain map
For animal movement visualization, I would usually use:
map.types = “Esri.WorldImagery”
for ecological interpretation (forest, rivers, habitat), and
map.types = “OpenStreetMap”
for teaching movement trajectories because roads and landmarks are easier to identify.

title: Interactive Time Series VIsualisation
text:
Data

data(srilanka_weekly_data)
colombo <- srilanka_weekly_data |>
  filter(district == "Colombo")

title: Interactive Time Series VIsualisation
text:
Interactive time series plot

library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)

colombo <- srilanka_weekly_data |>
  filter(district == "Colombo") |>
  mutate(start.date = mdy(start.date)) |>
  arrange(start.date)


library(xts)

Loading required package: zoo



Attaching package: 'zoo'


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    as.Date, as.Date.numeric



######################### Warning from 'xts' package ##########################

# The dplyr lag() function breaks how base R's lag() function is supposed to  #
# work, which breaks lag(my_xts). Calls to lag(my_xts) that you type or       #
# source() into this session won't work correctly.                            #

# dplyr from breaking base R's lag() function.                                #

# Set `options(xts.warn_dplyr_breaks_lag = FALSE)` to suppress this warning.  #
#                                                                             #
###############################################################################



Attaching package: 'xts'


The following objects are masked from 'package:dplyr':

    first, last

colombo_xts <- xts(
  colombo$cases,
  order.by = colombo$start.date
)


library(dygraphs)

dygraph(colombo_xts, main = "Colombo Weekly Dengue Cases") |>
  dyRangeSelector()

title: Untitled
text:
library("timetk")
library("tidyquant")
library(tidyverse)
library(denguedatahub)
library(anomalize)
library(tsibble)
library(fable)
library(fabletools) 
library(feasts) #STL decomposition





data(srilanka_weekly_data)
colombo <- srilanka_weekly_data |>
  filter(district == "Colombo")
# create a proper date index column
colombo <- srilanka_weekly_data |>
  filter(district == "Colombo") |>
  mutate(
    start.date = mdy(start.date)
  )





colombo |>
  plot_time_series(
    .date_var = start.date,
    .value = cases,
    .interactive = FALSE
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Weekly Dengue Cases in Colombo",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Cases"
  )

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the timetk package.
  Please report the issue at
  <https://github.com/business-science/timetk/issues>.


Ignoring unknown labels:
• colour : "Legend"










data(srilanka_weekly_data)
colombo <- srilanka_weekly_data |>
  filter(district == "Colombo")
# create a proper date index column
colombo <- srilanka_weekly_data |>
  filter(district == "Colombo") |>
  mutate(
    start.date = mdy(start.date)
  )





colombo |>
  plot_seasonal_diagnostics(
    .date_var = start.date,
    .value = cases,
    .interactive = FALSE
  )











What do you think about the plot? Is this correct?

colombo |>
  plot_stl_diagnostics(
    .date_var = start.date,
    .value = cases,
    .interactive = FALSE
  )

frequency = 13 observations per 1 quarter


trend = 53 observations per 1 year









Let’s fix the issue using tsibble and fabletools
What is wrong with the following code?

colombo_tsibble <- srilanka_weekly_data |>
  filter(district == "Colombo") |>
  mutate(
    start.date = mdy(start.date)
  ) |>
  arrange(start.date) |>
  as_tsibble(index = start.date)
colombo_tsibble

# A tsibble: 991 x 6 [1D]
    year  week start.date end.date   district cases
   <dbl> <dbl> <date>     <chr>      <chr>    <dbl>
 1  2006    52 2006-12-23 12/29/2006 Colombo     71
 2  2007     1 2006-12-30 1/5/2007   Colombo     40
 3  2007     2 2007-01-06 1/12/2007  Colombo     43
 4  2007     3 2007-01-13 1/19/2007  Colombo     38
 5  2007     4 2007-01-20 1/26/2007  Colombo     52
 6  2007     5 2007-01-27 2/2/2007   Colombo     69
 7  2007     6 2007-02-03 2/9/2007   Colombo     36
 8  2007     7 2007-02-10 2/16/2007  Colombo     42
 9  2007     8 2007-02-17 2/23/2007  Colombo     28
10  2007     9 2007-02-24 3/2/2007   Colombo     20
# ℹ 981 more rows


Let’s fix the issue

colombo_tsibble <- srilanka_weekly_data |>
  filter(district == "Colombo") |>
  mutate(
    start.date = mdy(start.date),
    year_week = yearweek(start.date)
  ) |>
  arrange(year_week) |>
  as_tsibble(index = year_week)
colombo_tsibble

# A tsibble: 991 x 7 [1W]
    year  week start.date end.date   district cases year_week
   <dbl> <dbl> <date>     <chr>      <chr>    <dbl>    <week>
 1  2006    52 2006-12-23 12/29/2006 Colombo     71  2006 W51
 2  2007     1 2006-12-30 1/5/2007   Colombo     40  2006 W52
 3  2007     2 2007-01-06 1/12/2007  Colombo     43  2007 W01
 4  2007     3 2007-01-13 1/19/2007  Colombo     38  2007 W02
 5  2007     4 2007-01-20 1/26/2007  Colombo     52  2007 W03
 6  2007     5 2007-01-27 2/2/2007   Colombo     69  2007 W04
 7  2007     6 2007-02-03 2/9/2007   Colombo     36  2007 W05
 8  2007     7 2007-02-10 2/16/2007  Colombo     42  2007 W06
 9  2007     8 2007-02-17 2/23/2007  Colombo     28  2007 W07
10  2007     9 2007-02-24 3/2/2007   Colombo     20  2007 W08
# ℹ 981 more rows

interval(colombo_tsibble)

<interval[1]>
[1] 1W



colombo_stl <- colombo_tsibble |>
  model(
    STL(cases ~ season(period = 52))
  )
colombo_stl

# A mable: 1 x 1
  `STL(cases ~ season(period = 52))`
                             <model>
1                              <STL>



colombo_components <- colombo_stl |>
  components()
colombo_components

# :        cases = trend + season_52 + remainder

 2 STL(cases ~ season(p…  2006 W52    40  22.9     14.0       3.10          26.0

 5 STL(cases ~ season(p…  2007 W03    52  23.7     20.4       7.92          31.6
 6 STL(cases ~ season(p…  2007 W04    69  24.0     18.6      26.4           50.4
 7 STL(cases ~ season(p…  2007 W05    36  24.2      4.55      7.21          31.5

 9 STL(cases ~ season(p…  2007 W07    28  24.8      1.73      1.47          26.3
10 STL(cases ~ season(p…  2007 W08    20  25.1    -18.4      13.3           38.4
# ℹ 981 more rows



colombo_components |>
  autoplot()

Ignoring unknown labels:
• colour : ".model"
• fill : ".model"









Let’s fix the issue with timetk

colombo %>%
  plot_stl_diagnostics(
    .date_var = start.date,
    .value = cases,
    .frequency = 52,
    .trend = 104,
    .interactive = FALSE
  )

frequency = 52 observations


trend = 104 observations













colombo %>%
  time_decompose(
    cases,
    method = "stl",
    frequency = 52
  ) %>%
  anomalize(
    remainder,
    method = "iqr"
  ) %>%
  time_recompose() %>%
  plot_anomalies(time_recomposed = TRUE)

Registered S3 method overwritten by 'tibbletime':
  method  from
  seq.hms hms

trend = 52 weeks

colombo |>
  plot_time_series(
    .date_var = start.date,
    .value = cases,
    .color_var = year(start.date),
    .interactive = TRUE
  )

Warning: There were 5 warnings in `dplyr::mutate()`.
The first warning was:
ℹ In argument: `.value_smooth = auto_smooth(...)`.
ℹ In group 1: `.color_mod = 2006`.
Caused by warning in `simpleLoess()`:
! span too small.   fewer data values than degrees of freedom.
ℹ Run `dplyr::last_dplyr_warnings()` to see the 4 remaining warnings.






or

library(ggplot2)

colombo |>
  mutate(
    Year = year(start.date),
    Week = isoweek(start.date)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(Week, factor(Year), fill = cases)) +
  geom_tile() +
  labs(
    title = "Weekly Dengue Cases Heatmap",
    x = "Week",
    y = "Year"
  )












library(dplyr)
library(slider)
library(timetk)

colombo |>
  mutate(
    MA52 = slide_dbl(cases,
                     mean,
                     .before = 51,
                     .complete = TRUE)
  ) |>
  plot_time_series(
    .date_var = start.date,
    .value = cases,
    .interactive = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_line(aes(y = MA52), color = "blue")

colombo %>%
  plot_stl_diagnostics(
    .date_var = start.date,
    .value = cases,
    .frequency = 52,
    .trend = 104,
    .interactive = FALSE
  )

frequency = 52 observations


trend = 104 observations

title: Untitled
text:
colombo %>%
  time_decompose(
    cases,
    method = "stl",
    frequency = 52
  ) %>%
  anomalize(
    remainder,
    method = "iqr"
  ) %>%
  time_recompose() %>%
  plot_anomalies(time_recomposed = TRUE)

or

library(ggplot2)

colombo |>
  mutate(
    Year = year(start.date),
    Week = isoweek(start.date)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(Week, factor(Year), fill = cases)) +
  geom_tile() +
  labs(
    title = "Weekly Dengue Cases Heatmap",
    x = "Week",
    y = "Year"
  )

title: Untitled
text:
library(dplyr)
library(slider)
library(timetk)

colombo |>
  mutate(
    MA52 = slide_dbl(cases,
                     mean,
                     .before = 51,
                     .complete = TRUE)
  ) |>
  plot_time_series(
    .date_var = start.date,
    .value = cases,
    .interactive = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_line(aes(y = MA52), color = "blue")

title: Other plots
text:
library(tidyverse)


library(ggforce)
ggplot(iris, aes(Petal.Length, Petal.Width, colour = Species)) +
    geom_point() +
    facet_zoom(x = Species == "versicolor")









library("ggalt")

Registered S3 methods overwritten by 'ggalt':
  method                  from   
  grobHeight.absoluteGrob ggplot2
  grobWidth.absoluteGrob  ggplot2
  grobX.absoluteGrob      ggplot2
  grobY.absoluteGrob      ggplot2

circle.df <- iris |> filter(Species == "setosa")
ggplot(iris, aes(Petal.Length, Petal.Width)) +
    geom_point(aes(colour = Species)) + 
  geom_encircle(data = circle.df, linetype = 2)









library(ggpubr)
# Grouped Scatter plot with marginal density plots
ggscatterhist(
  iris, x = "Sepal.Length", y = "Sepal.Width",
  color = "Species", size = 3, alpha = 0.6,
  palette = c("#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "#FC4E07"),
  margin.params = list(fill = "Species", color = "black", size = 0.2)
  )

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the ggpubr package.
  Please report the issue at <https://github.com/kassambara/ggpubr/issues>.










# Use box plot as marginal plots
ggscatterhist(
  iris, x = "Sepal.Length", y = "Sepal.Width",
  color = "Species", size = 3, alpha = 0.6,
  palette = c("#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "#FC4E07"),
  margin.plot = "boxplot",
  ggtheme = theme_bw()
  )

title: STA 492 2.0/ ASP 460 2.0 Data Visualisation
text:
Datasets

Pedestrian counts in Melbourne city

library(sugrrants)
data(hourly_peds)

Tickets issued for parking violations in the city of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania in 2017

library(tidyverse)

philly <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/master/data/2019/2019-12-03/tickets.csv")

Emission data

emissions <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/master/data/2024/2024-05-21/emissions.csv')
emissions
4 ToothGrowth
ToothGrowth
Take a suitable visualization to illustrate the relationship between supp, dose, and the mean tooth growth

Sydney Beaches: Water Quality Reliability

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
if (!require("pacman")) install.packages("pacman")
pacman::p_load(
  tidytuesdayR,
  tidyverse,    
  ggtext,        
  showtext,      
  janitor,       
  scales,        
  glue         
  )
})
tt <- tidytuesdayR::tt_load(2025, week = 20)

water_quality_raw <- tt$water_quality |> clean_names()
weather_raw <- tt$weather |> clean_names()

tidytuesdayR::readme(tt)
rm(tt)

Common plots for quantitative data with qualitative variables
In-Class Exercise:
Recreate imag1.jpeg and img2.jpeg. Identify the drawbacks of the given chart, if any, and suggest suitable improvements to make the visualization clearer, more effective, and easier to interpret.
Data source for image 1: https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/databrowser/view/edat_aes_l21/default/table?lang=en&category=educ.educ_lang.educ_lang_00.edat_aes_l2

title: STA 492 2.0/ ASP 460 2.0 Data Visualisation
text:
Mid evaluation - Mini Project
Assignment announced date: 2 June 2026
Use Sri Lanka dengue data from the denguedatahub package to develop an interactive dashboard for exploring dengue situation in Sri Lanka. In addition, prepare a short report explaining the data, the methods used for analysis (such as trend and seasonality exploration), and the key findings, including any observed patterns or outbreak periods.
Due date: ~17 June 2026~ 10 July 2026
Please upload all your documents as a single file to the “Mid Evaluation” tab in Google Classroom. Ensure that the file is named using your index number.

Link to data: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-06-02/eplp.csv
library(tidytuesday)
data <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-06-02/eplp.csv') |> 
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~replace_values(.x, -98 ~ NA)))
